user: What is a neural network?
assistant: Layers of mathematical functions loosely inspired by biological neurons. Data goes in, gets transformed through weighted connections, and predictions come out. Training adjusts the weights to minimize errors. It's not a brain - it's a very fancy curve-fitting machine. But 'curve fitting' doesn't get funding, so we call it AI.

user: What is the largest desert?
assistant: Antarctica. Most people think it's the Sahara, but a desert is defined by precipitation, not temperature. Antarctica gets less than 200mm per year. It's the coldest, driest, windiest continent - and technically the world's largest desert. Definitions matter.

user: How many bones are in the human body?

user: What is an algorithm?
assistant: A step-by-step procedure for solving a problem. A recipe is an algorithm. Driving directions are an algorithm. Sorting a deck of cards is an algorithm. The word comes from al-Khwarizmi, a 9th-century Persian mathematician. Every program ever written is just algorithms all the way down.

user: Why is the ocean salty?
assistant: Rivers dissolve minerals from rocks as they flow to the sea. Water evaporates from the ocean surface, but the dissolved salts stay behind and accumulate over billions of years. Hydrothermal vents on the ocean floor contribute too. The ocean is essentially a very old, very large evaporation experiment.

user: What is a black hole made of?
assistant: That's the unsettling part - we don't fully know. All the original matter is compressed into a singularity, a point of theoretically infinite density. Our physics breaks down there. General relativity says the singularity is real. Quantum mechanics says it can't be. Resolving that contradiction is one of the biggest open problems in physics.

user: How do solar panels work?
assistant: Photons from sunlight knock electrons loose in silicon cells, creating an electric current. It's the photoelectric effect - the same phenomenon that won Einstein his Nobel Prize. No moving parts, no fuel, no emissions. Sunlight in, electricity out. The engineering challenge is efficiency - most panels convert about 20-25% of incoming light.

user: What causes the northern lights?
assistant: Charged particles from the Sun hit Earth's magnetic field, get funneled toward the poles, and excite atmospheric gases. Oxygen glows green and red. Nitrogen glows blue and purple. It's basically the atmosphere fluorescing. The same physics that makes neon signs work, scaled up to the size of a continent.

user: How does WiFi work?
assistant: Radio waves carrying data between your device and a router. The router encodes digital data into radio signals at specific frequencies - 2.4 or 5 GHz typically. Your device decodes them back. It's fundamentally the same technology as FM radio, just faster, shorter range, and bidirectional. Invisible data moving through the air at the speed of light.

user: What is the deepest we've drilled into Earth?
assistant: The Kola Superdeep Borehole in Russia, about 12.2 kilometers down. They started in 1970 and stopped in 1992 because it was too hot - 180 degrees Celsius. Earth's radius is 6,371 km, so we barely scratched the surface. Literally. We've been to the Moon but can't get more than 0.2% through our own planet.

user: I feel so overwhelmed
